subject: Statistics
title: Why the Bell Curve Appears Everywhere
description: A fast breakdown of Why the Bell Curve Appears Everywhere for builders.

hook.text: Roll one die: every outcome is equally likely. Roll a hundred and average them: a bell curve appears.

buildup.text: The Central Limit Theorem explains why. Average enough random values and the result always looks normal.

discovery.text: It doesn't matter what the original data looks like. Averages of large samples converge to a bell curve.

twist.text: This is why polls work. A sample of 1,000 people can represent millions — thanks to the bell curve.

climax.text: The Central Limit Theorem is arguably the most important result in all of statistics.

punchline.text: Average enough chaos and order always appears.

quiz.question: What does the Central Limit Theorem guarantee?
options: Averages of large samples converge to a normal distribution regardless of the original shape
Larger samples always have smaller means
